coredata.dc:title: Tunable N-doped carbon dots/SnO2 interface as a stable artificial solid electrolyte interphase for high-performance aqueous zinc-ion batteries

coredata.dc:description: Poor stability of zinc (Zn) anode hinders the use of aqueous zinc-ion batteries (AZIBs) for large-scale energy storage. Here, we report an effective artificial solid electrolyte interphase (ASEI) using N-doped carbon dots (CDs) and SnO2 to stabilize Zn anodes. By optimizing the CDs/SnO2 ratio, we can synthesize porous composites to construct "bayberry" and flower-like morphologies. The N-doped CDs/SnO2 anode creates surface dipoles and changes in charge distribution, allowing Zn ions to move to nitrogen functionalized sites with reduced adsorption barriers. Furthermore, hydroxyl oxygen boosts the surface's hydrophilicity, resulting in stronger adhesion to the Zn anode and better ion accessibility. This generates dense nucleation sites for uniform Zn deposition. The CDs/SnO2@Zn electrode achieves a low nucleation potential of 47 mV and maintains 99.6 % coulombic efficiency (CE) over 1000 cycles at 2 mA cm−2. In the symmetrical cells, the modified Zn anode exhibits stable cycling for 1200 h at 1 mAh cm−2. A full cell with CDs/SnO2@Zn anode and MnO2 cathode retains 96.6 % capacity after 800 h. This study introduces a promising strategy for stabilizing Zn anodes and offers valuable insights for designing dendrite-free electrodes in next-generation AZIBs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the nitrogen doped CDs/SnO2 tuning interface strategy, (b) X-ray diffraction patterns of pristine SnO2, CDs/SnO2-1 and CDs/SnO2-2 and (c-f) XPS spectra of Sn, O, C and N elements present in the CDs/SnO2-1.

Morphological analysis of SnO2 and CDs/SnO2: (a, b) FE-SEM images of pristine SnO2, (c,d) Nitrogen doped CDs/SnO2, (e, f) HR-TEM images of CDs/SnO2, (g, h) Fourier transform SAED pattern and lattice spacing of CDs/SnO2 and (i-l) EDS mappings for tin (Sn), oxygen (O), nitrogen (N), and carbon (C).

(a) Schematic illustration of electrochemical examinations of CDs/SnO2-1@Zn, (b) Plating/stripping cycles of bare-Zn and CDs/SnO2-1@Zn symmetric cells at a current density of 2 mA cm–2 and an aerial capacity of 1 mA h cm–2. (c) Initial cycling bare-Zn and CDs/SnO2-1@Zn, (d) The symmetric cells' rate performance was as follows: current density rose gradually from 0.5 to 5 mA cm–2, then returned to 1 mA cm–2 for the remaining cycles, (e) Voltage profiles of the bare-Zn and CDs/SnO2-1@Zn, (f) Cycling voltammogram curves at a scan rate of 2 mV s–1 and (g) Coulombic efficiency of Zn plating/stripping at a current density of 2 mA cm−2.

(a) Schematic illustration of plating/stripping of CDs/SnO2-1@Zn, (b) Surficial (top) and cross-sectional (bottom) SEM images of bare Zn and CDs/SnO2-1@Zn, (c) Surface analysis: In situ optical microscope of bare Zn (top) and CDs/SnO2-1@Zn (bottom), (d) Contact angle images of ZnSO4 droplet on bare Zn and CDs/SnO2-1@Zn anode, (e) Tafel curves of Zn//Zn cells and CDs/SnO2-1@Zn, (f) HER plots of bare Zn and CDs/SnO2-1@Zn (insert is the corresponding Tafel plots) and (g and h) XANES and XFAS spectrum of CDs/SnO2-1@Zn anode after 100 cycling.

Electrochemical performances of full cells: (a) CV curves @ 0.2 mV s−1 and insert, schematic representation of working mechanism of CDs/SnO2@Zn ‖ δ-MnO2. (b) Galvanostatic charge/discharge curves of both bare Zn and CDs/SnO2-1@Zn based cells at a current density of 1 A g−1. (c) Galvanostatic charge/discharge profiles at different cycles, (d) Rate performance of the Zn and CDs/SnO2-1@Zn based cells and (e) Long cycling performance at 2 A g–1.

head.ce:title: Tunable N-doped carbon dots/SnO2 interface as a stable artificial solid electrolyte interphase for high-performance aqueous zinc-ion batteries

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Poor stability of zinc (Zn) anode hinders the use of aqueous zinc-ion batteries (AZIBs) for large-scale energy storage. Here, we report an effective artificial solid electrolyte interphase (ASEI) using N-doped carbon dots (CDs) and SnO2 to stabilize Zn anodes. By optimizing the CDs/SnO2 ratio, we can synthesize porous composites to construct "bayberry" and flower-like morphologies. The N-doped CDs/SnO2 anode creates surface dipoles and changes in charge distribution, allowing Zn ions to move to nitrogen functionalized sites with reduced adsorption barriers. Furthermore, hydroxyl oxygen boosts the surface's hydrophilicity, resulting in stronger adhesion to the Zn anode and better ion accessibility. This generates dense nucleation sites for uniform Zn deposition. The CDs/SnO2@Zn electrode achieves a low nucleation potential of 47 mV and maintains 99.6 % coulombic efficiency (CE) over 1000 cycles at 2 mA cm−2. In the symmetrical cells, the modified Zn anode exhibits stable cycling for 1200 h at 1 mAh cm−2. A full cell with CDs/SnO2@Zn anode and MnO2 cathode retains 96.6 % capacity after 800 h. This study introduces a promising strategy for stabilizing Zn anodes and offers valuable insights for designing dendrite-free electrodes in next-generation AZIBs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Aqueous zinc-ion batteries (AZIBs) have emerged as promising candidates for large-scale energy storage due to their high safety, cost-effectiveness, and environmental friendliness [1–3]. The abundance of Zn, its low redox potential (-0.762 V vs. SHE), and low toxicity make it an attractive anode material [4,5]. However, the practical application of AZIBs is hindered by the erratic cyclability of the Zn anode, leading to Zn dendrite formation and detrimental side reactions [6]. Zn dendrites, formed during repeated plating/stripping cycles, cause rapid capacity fading and potential separator failure [6,7]. The detachment of these dendrites during stripping creates "dead zinc," resulting in poor coulombic efficiency (CE) [8–10]. It is significant that the slow ion transport kinetics during Zn plating/stripping leads to hydrogen evolution reactions (HER) and corrosion, greatly impairing AZIB performance [10–12]. Recent studies have highlighted the critical role of Zn2+ solvation structures in regulating dendrite formation and side reactions, emphasizing the need for strategies that can effectively control these interfacial processes [13,14]. Various strategies have been proposed to address these challenges, including enhancing the structure of the Zn anode [15,16], using electrolyte additives [17–19], creating artificial solid electrolyte interface layers (ASEIs) [20], and modifying electrolyte compositions. Protective materials, such as metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [21,22], covalent organic frameworks (COFs) [5,23,24], zeolite nanoparticles [25], organic polymers [26,27], and inorganic compounds [28–30] have been investigated. However, most of these approaches rely on single-function ASEI architectures, only partially addressing the issues.

It is noted that SnO2 materials have a huge impact on their widespread adoption as anode host materials for energy storage devices. However, the practical application of SnO2 is hindered by its sporadic electronic conductivity and intrinsic brittleness [31]. Such limitations often lead to poor electrical performance and mechanical instability, during charge-discharge cycles [32]. Because of its high energy density, SnO2 relies on metal valence fluctuations during redox reactions, but some natural limitations, such as weaker electrical conductivities plus volume change are affected during charge-discharge cycles. To overcome these shortcomings, various carbonaceous components into SnO2-based materials, such as graphite, carbon nanotubes, and graphene nanotubes have been introduced. The distinct spatial arrangement and voids of these nanomaterials enable them to have a potent buffering impact on Sn accumulation and volume expansion. Improved metal ion transport kinetics are made possible by the void area these materials provide, which can also adjust the structural strain. By combining N-doped carbon dots (CDs) with SnO2, we aim to leverage the advantages of both materials while mitigating their individual limitations.

In this work, we propose an effective approach using N-doped CDs and SnO2 to create a tunable interface for Zn anode stabilization. CDs, carbon nanostructures smaller than 10 nm, offer exceptional chemical stability and high specific surface areas [33]. Their greater surface area allows them to accommodate multi-functional groups for surface wettability, creating larger interface areas between CDs and other materials [34]. Utilizing N-doped CDs/SnO2 allows for precise control of metal compound nucleation and growth, resulting in an optimized microstructure and morphology [35]. This approach creates a synergistic relationship between dopants, hydrophilicity, and nucleation dynamics within the CDs/SnO2 layer, effectively regulating Zn deposition and inhibiting side reactions. Consequently, Zn plating/stripping kinetics are enhanced, suppressing detrimental side reactions (HER) while maintaining the long-term stability of the Zn anode. As a result, The CDs/SnO₂@Zn//SnO₂@Zn cell with a carbon dot layer electrolyte has excellent wettability, electrical conductivity, and cyclic stability. It can operate for over 2000 h at 2 mA cm⁻²/0.5 mAh cm⁻². The CDs/SnO2@Zn//δ-MnO2 cell performs superbly over 800 h, with an outstanding average coulombic efficiency of 98.3 %. This study not only introduces a new material combination for Zn anode protection but also provides mechanistic insights through detailed characterization and analysis. Our findings have the potential to greatly advance the development of high-performance, long-lasting AZIBs for practical energy storage applications.

In Fig. 1, citric acid (CA) and ethylene diamine tetra acetic acid (EDTA) are hydrothermally treated to form N-doped CDs via an autoclave. After the N-doped CDs solution is obtained, SnCl4·5 H2O is added with different molarity to synthesis CDs/SnO2-1 and CDs/SnO2-2, followed by an ethanol wash. It is noted that a clear distinction between the morphologies and structures of tuned N-doped CDs/SnO2, is shown. Besides, the ratio of CDs can yield a morphology similar to bay berries and flowers. To construct multi-morphologies with different ratios of CDs is a simple, straightforward and cost-effective technique, when compared to other methods, such as the hard and soft template method, chemical-deposition method and other ways, as described in the literature. Since the surficial hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on CDs have a negative charge, they can initially absorb Sn4+ onto their surface by electrostatic contact. Next, the absorbed Sn4+ can be converted into numerous SnO2 nuclei at the onset of the hydrothermal reaction. Finally, the agglomeration effect from CDs causes both cross-link and isotropic coalescence-growth between various multi-CDs/SnO2 particles as the hydrothermal reaction progresses [36]. Therefore, because of surface energy, the CDs/SnO2 composites have an almost spherical appearance; the multi CDs/SnO2 particles among them resemble bayberry structures [33].

In Fig. 1b, the XRD patterns of pristine SnO2 and CDs/SnO2 hybrid 1 and 2 are presented. Typical peaks found for the two CDs are 26.8°, 34.96°, 38.17° 52.17° 54.91°, and 65.76°. These peaks are in agreement with the (110), (101), (200), (211), (002), and (301) planes of the tetragonal rutile SnO2 structure (JCPDS Card No. 41–1445). Compared to pristine SnO2, the N-doped CDs/SnO2 hybrid shows a notable expansion at approximately 26.8°, with the peak's width at half-height changing from 1.5° to 2.1°. This expansion indicates the presence of carbon material (5–20°) [36]. The N-doped CD XRD spectrum is displayed in Fig. S1.

Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was used to determine the SnO2 content of the samples. In Fig. S2, a noticeable two-stage weight loss between 250 and 900 °C is observed due to CD consumption in the air atmosphere. Consequently, only the SnO2 in the CDs/SnO2 composites remains. The pristine SnO2 sample also displayed mass loss due to the bonded water. Above 200 °C, carbonization occurs, indicating that surface functional groups are denatured, resulting in an N-functionalized graphitic carbon surface. TGA analysis demonstrated an 11 % weight loss in CDs/SnO2-1 between 200 and 600 °C due to functional group decomposition and graphitic carbon formation, which could prevent oxidative degradation. This outcome is attributed to the carbon loss from the CDs/SnO2 samples in the ambient air.

In Figs. S3 and 4, to clarify the doping of CDs, both band energy and absorption spectra of the N-doped CDs and the CDs/SnO2 hybrid are displayed. The presence of the n-π* transition of CO is demonstrated by the absorption peak for CDs, which is centered at 330 nm and shifted to 300 nm for CDs/SnO2, depending on CDs ratios [37,38]. On the hybrid surfaces, these functional groups can produce flaws and a large number of reaction sites. In-depth analysis of the composition of the as-prepared composites was conducted, using Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy [35]. In Fig. S5, a peak at around 625 cm−1, attributed to the Sn-O stretching modes of SnO2, CDs/SnO2-1 and SnO2, CDs/SnO2-2, is observed, confirming the successful synthesis of SnO2.

To analyze the synthesized N-doped CDs/SnO2 composites with tunable morphologies [39], Field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) investigation reveals how the addition of CDs affects the chemical and physical properties of CDs, CDs/SnO2-1 and CDs/SnO2-2. In Fig. 2 and S6, a significant difference in particle size and morphology of hybrid CDs/SnO2-1 and CDs/SnO2-2, when compared to pure SnO2, is seen. How much Sn and CDs are synthesized can determine the N- doped CDs/SnO2 morphologies. The hybrid CDs/SnO2-1 and CDs/SnO2-2 have various surface textures but are generally homogeneous spheres with diameters ranging from 1 to 1.2 μm. When a small quantity of CDs (0.3 mg/mL) is added, the manufacture of N-doped CDs/SnO2 results in nanoballs that are 50–70 nm long, exhibiting CDs/SnO2-1 a bayberry-like appearance. These nanoballs disappear. However, when an additional amount of CDs (0.6 mg/mL) is added, the CDs/SnO2 morphology reveals a sharp blooming. The berry structure is formed by an even combination of N-CDs and SnO2 particles, as demonstrated by the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) image of the N-CDs/SnO2-1.

It is worth noting that N-doped CDs nanoballs are used to produce both surface plates and interior spheres. However, CDs with a graphitized lattice are primarily found along the composite's edge, indicating that CDs preferentially reside on the composite's surface, filling the gaps between SnO2 nanoparticles. FE-SEM and HR-TEM images of pristine N-doped CDs are illustrated in Fig. S6 and S7 for comparison. Such a structure enables CDs to manage the electrolyte suitably when used as electrode materials. During the plating-stripping processes, the functional groups of the CDs are seen to contribute to Zn solvation and the de-solvation structure, thereby enhancing Zn2+ kinetics [40,41]. In Fig. S8, according to the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method, the predicted surface areas of CDs/SnO2-1, and CDs/SnO2-2 are found to be 185.42, and 130.14 cm3 g−1, respectively. Upon reaching 400°C, the -OH, -COOH and -NH2 functional groups of CDs undergo carbonization, thus forming N-functionalized graphic carbon surface. This functionalized carbon structure, which incorporates both carbon and nitrogen in a highly thermal stable, layered configuration, imparts it with excellent stability and promising electrochemical activity [42]. It is also seen that when the ratio of CDs increases, the CDs are eliminated from the composite, leaving a homogeneous mesoporous distribution in the SnO2 framework, inducing a sudden decrease in its specific surface area.

In Fig. S9, investigating the chemical valence of the elements as well as the interaction between CDs and SnO2, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out. In Fig. 1c and d, the presence of Sn, O, N and C is confirmed by a complete survey scan. Hence, the peaks at 487.2 and 496.3 eV correspond to Sn4+, as determined by XPS. The O 1 s spectra of pure SnO2 are classified into two categories of oxygen states [43]. The peak at 531.8 eV is attributed to Sn-OH, demonstrating the presence of Sn (OH)4 in the sample. Another peak at 529.6 eV belongs to Sn-O-C bonds, which cannot be found in the pristine SnO2. The electron density at O sites is enhanced by increased Sn-O-C bonds, suggesting that additional CDs can increase the amount of Sn-O-C bonds and explain the increase in binding energy. During Zn2+ plating and stripping, the Sn-O-C bonds can break and re-form in a reversible and lasting manner [36]. Further, the Sn-OH peaks are seen to appear at 533.2 eV.

In Fig. 1e, the high-resolution C 1 s spectra of CDs/SnO2-1 are displayed. Matching peaks of CDs around 283.89, 285.75, 287.26, and 289.32 eV correspond to C-C/CC, C-O/C-N, CO, and OC-O, respectively. Primary peaks in the N 1 s area are assigned to pyrrolic N and pyridinic N and, respectively, with binding energies of 401.5, and 398.7 eV, respectively (Fig. 1f). Pyrrolic N can act as a dopant and is able to alter local electronic structure in such a way that it can tune the morphology and accelerate Zn2+ [44]. In Fig. S10, both the CDs XPS spectrum and the related deconvolution spectra for C, N, and O are shown.

In Fig. 3a, the galvanostatic cycling of the built symmetrical cells in 2.0 M ZnSO4 aqueous electrolyte is used to study the electrochemical stability of the manufactured anodes during the Zn plating/stripping process. In Fig. 3b and c, the cycling stability of symmetrical cells made from bare Zn and CDs/SnO2@Zn, using a current density of 2.0 mA cm−2, are compared. Results reveal that CDs/SnO2@Zn exhibit superior electrochemical stability over bare Zn, cycling up to 1200 h. However, after 200 h, bare Zn underwent a short circuit, resulting in cell failure.

While the initial Zn kinetics, such as plating and stripping, are favorable, uncontrolled electrochemical activity for more than 200 cycles induces Zn dendrite formation, corrosion, and hydrogen evolution. The half-cell collapses after 200 cycles, and it is clear that CDs/SnO₂@Zn has a higher Coulombic efficiency. This enhanced efficiency is attributed to the reduced accumulation of side products at the electrode-electrolyte interface, which hinders charge transmission in the bare Zn cell. On the other hand, the CDs/SnO₂@Zn anode exhibits higher Coulombic efficiency in a symmetric cell due to enhanced Zn diffusion along the CDs/SnO₂@Zn anode surface. The addition of O and N polar functional groups to CDs/SnO₂@Zn ensures a more uniform and stable Zn deposition by enhancing the adhesion between the substrate and the Zn-deposited layer. Consequently, the symmetrical CDs/SnO₂@Zn cells demonstrate robust plating and stripping processes.

Fig. 3b illustrates that the Zn deposition process with CDs/SnO₂@Zn is highly reversible, as the Zn deposition plateaus in cycles 100, 200, and 300 overlap considerably. Conversely, Zn deposition plateaus on bare Zn fluctuate with cycling, and the deposition polarization is significantly greater than that of CDs/SnO₂@Zn. This stable deposition potential further demonstrates that the dopants in CDs/SnO₂@Zn are exceptionally stable and that the metal's surface morphology is preserved due to its excellent substrate adherence; neither particle detachment nor dopant consumption was observed.

In Fig. S11b, CDs/SnO2-1@Zn can operate up to 500 cycles, at this high current density (5 mA cm−2). Nevertheless, the bare Zn anode can only reach about 100 cycles. The reason for bare Zn's failure can be attributed to the fast creation and growth of dendrites at high current densities, which can cause short circuits and separator punctures. It is significant that overpotential in the CDs/SnO2-1@Zn anode is approximately 100 mV, which is much lower than bare Zn at the high current density. In addition, the anode has stability that is roughly three times longer than bare Zn anodes. In Fig. S11c (EIS value in symmetric cells), CDs/SnO2-1@Zn showed lower charge transfer resistance (Rct) than bare Zn, demonstrating that CDs could improve Zn2+ conductivity.

In Fig. 3d, Zn plating/stripping experiments are conducted on assembled symmetrical cells made of bare Zn and CDs/SnO2-1@Zn, with a fixed capacity of 1.0 mAh cm−2 and current densities, ranging from 0.5 to 5 mA cm−2. The symmetrical CDs/SnO2-1@Zn cells demonstrate improved performance for all current densities. CDs/SnO2-1@Zn exhibits reduced polarization voltage, resulting in potential loops for the symmetrical CDs/SnO2-1@Zn cells at 5 mA cm−2.

In Fig. 3e, for the bare Zn electrode, overpotential value stayed within 79 mV, indicating decreased nucleation resistance and high reaction kinetics in the CDs/SnO2@Zn anode. This value proved to be higher than that of the Zn symmetric cell in the CDs samples. After 400 h cycling, the symmetric bare Zn cells failed. The potential hysteresis of this sample was greater than that of bare Zn samples. Thus, electrode kinetics and rate capability of the CDs/SnO2-1@Zn anodes are markedly enhanced in comparison to the bare Zn. It is acknowledged that CDs not only prevent interfacial side reactions but also encourage consistent Zn deposition, inhibiting the growth of Zn dendrites. The initial nucleation behavior of Zn deposition during electrodeposition has a substantial impact on the functional group of electrodes. To clarify the effect of CDs/SnO2-1 on Zn nucleation, nucleation overpotential (NOP) was investigated. In Fig. 3e and f, the Zn//Cu cells with the CDs/SnO2-1 electrolyte display increased NOP, indicating a stronger driving force for Zn nucleation. As a result of the preferential adsorption of CDs at the Zn-metal interface during hydrated Zn2+ de-solvation in the deposition process, nucleation barriers increased. This process slows down the rapid kinetics of Zn reduction and promotes uniform fine-nuclei deposition behavior. Moreover, as shown by EIS data, the Zn-metal interface Zn2+ electrochemical reduction kinetics are modulated by CDs/SnO2-1 (Fig. S11c).

In Fig. S11c, the EIS curves of Zn-symmetric cells during several cycles with CDs/SnO2-1 and CDs/SnO2-2 are shown. Zn deposition occurs every three min, and each cycle is made up of these repetitions. In the Zn-Zn cells with the pure electrolyte, the charge transfer resistance (Rct) progressively drops over cumulative deposition time. The main cause of this is the uneven Zn deposition brought on by the quick electrochemical reduction kinetics of Zn2+. After a few cycles, the symmetrical cells that use an electrolyte containing CDs/SnO2-1 quickly stabilize Rct. The capacity of CDs/SnO2-1 to control Zn2+ electrochemical reduction kinetics and guarantee uniform Zn deposition is validated by consistent EIS measurements throughout several plating/stripping cycles. Results obtained clearly indicate that the electrochemical performance of the CDs/SnO2-1@Zn anode is superior. Their performance is comparable to that previously carried out without Zn anode modifications; the CDs/SnO2-1@Zn anode enhanced the electrochemical capability [45].

In Fig. 4a, the desolvation mechanism of CDs/SnO2-1@Zn with uniform Zn deposition of anode, is illustrated. To confirm these findings, we investigated the morphological evolution (FE-SEM) and crystal structure (XRD) of Zn anodes following cycling. After 100 cycles in the Zn anode, distinct diffraction peaks of by-products (ZnSO4.(OH)6. 2H2O) are detected in the bare Zn [46]. In contrast, even after 800 cycles with an electrolyte containing CDs/SnO2-1, no such diffraction peaks are visible in the Zn anode. In Fig. S12, after subsequent cycles, the increased relative peak intensity in comparison to the (101) plane indicates the dominance of the (002) plane of Zn deposition [47,48]. The production of Zn dendrites is greatly reduced by the preferential deposition on the Zn (002) plane.

In Fig. 4b, the efficacy of the CDs in inhibiting dendrite growth on the anode surface is confirmed by obtaining ex situ SEM images of the CDs/SnO2-1@Zn anodes. Such anodes were obtained after cycling 50 h in symmetrical cells at a current density of 1.0 mA cm−2. Large, bulky dendrites formed in all directions on the surface of the bare Zn anode (Fig. 4b LHS). Zn deposition in CDs/SnO2-1@Zn occurred in ordered horizontal directions (Fig. 4b RHS). Due to the surface's low wettability and few Zn nucleation sites, Zn2+ ions are not uniformly deposited on bare Zn, which accounts for these observations.

Further, the wettability of the CDs anode, combined with its protective role against side reactions, leads to more Zn nucleation regions and controlled deposition of Zn2+ ions across the surface area of anode. This results in uniform Zn electrodeposition and longer cycling lifespan [49]. As shown in Fig. 4c, hydrogen production during the Zn plating/stripping process was observed on the bare Zn anode surface, as shown by in-situ optical microscopy. No gas or bubble formation was found in CDs/SnO2-1@Zn. The bare Zn had a smooth surface, but after 10 min, mossy-looking deposits of Zn appear and progressively grow into enormous dendrites. Hard dendrite growth increases the possibility of battery short circuits due to separator penetration, lowering CE. The uneven distribution of electrolyte when it reaches the anode surface contributes significantly to dendritic growth. Uneven electrodepositions can occur from the non-homogeneous distribution of Zn2+ ions caused by this. The anode surface's wettability is a hydroxyl oxygen in preventing dendrite formation and parasitic side reactions [50]. It is significant that the wettability of the anode surface has a powerful impact on minimizing dendrite formation and parasitic reactions [51].

The wettability of as-prepared anodes with 1.0 M ZnSO4 aqueous electrolyte was assessed, using the dynamic contact angle measured in the ambient environment. In Fig. 4d, the contact angle between the bare Zn electrode is 85.2° while the contact angle between the electrolyte and CDs is 60.3°. The lower angle observed at the CDs/SnO2@Zn electrolyte interface suggests an increase in interfacial hydrophilicity. This angle is lower because the structure of CDs includes polar hydroxyl and pyrrolic N functional groups that interact with water molecules. Such a structure proves advantageous for promoting constant Zn deposition over the course of protracted cycling. The CDs material is zincophilic. Consequently, the final modified anode's wettability is much higher than that of bare Zn. During electrochemical processes, electrode corrosion and HER are two potential harmful reactions that can happen. In Fig. 4e and f, corrosion and HER reaction of bare Zn and CDs/SnO2-1@Zn electrodes undergo linear polarization analysis. Upon the application of CDs/SnO2-1@Zn, corrosion potential increases from −0.97 to −0.93 V, and corrosion current density decreased from 3.89 to 3.14 mA cm−2. These findings support the notion that homogenous Zn nucleation and deposition are encouraged by the CDs/SnO2-1@Zn interphase. In addition, the interface of CDs is seen to prevent. In LSV curves, CDs/SnO2@Zn has a negative HER onset potential compared to bare Zn; due to interfacial ionic effects, water-related HER is significantly suppressed [51]. The LSV-determined Tafel slope in CDs/SnO2@Zn is 258 mV dec−1, which is greater than in bare Zn (163 mV dec−1), indicates a decrease in the HER rate due to the N-doped CDs interface [52]. Tafel and LSV studies signifies that CDs/SnO2@Zn exhibits higher suppression of Zn corrosion and HER. These finding underscores stability and electrochemical performance of Zn anodes with the hybrid SEI.

To describe Zn speciation on the electrode surface, after Zn was deposited on the anode for 1 h at a current density of 1 mA cm−2, ex situ XAS was carried out, at a grazing incident angle of 0.3°. In Fig. 4g and h, XANES and EXAFS spectra at Zn K-edge were measured by detecting the matching fluorescence signal associated with Zn speciation. In the Zn anode without CDs/SnO2-1, Zn (002) features are noted. In Fig. 4g, a greater energy, which is very close to the absorption edge of Zn2+ species in standard ZnSO4, is observed at the Zn K-edge of the XANES spectra with the addition of Zn@CDs/SnO2-1. This outcome indicates the interaction of Zn2+ on the CDs/SnO2-1 layer: the hydrated ZnSO4 is seen to have a coordination number of 6 [53]. Greater interaction between the deposited Zn and CD/SnO2-1 is demonstrated more than that of Zn2+solvation. Subsequently, the OH– ions in the solvent can interact with the Zn2+ cations, ZnSO4 salt, and H2O molecules, forming the sulfate hydroxide complex on the electrode surface. Such a byproduct is consistent with a previous report [49].

In Fig. 4h, the first-shell scattering of Zn species has changed, highlighting the local coordination with respect to the EXAFS data, shown in the radial distance distribution (R-space). Similar to the typical compositions ZnSO4.(OH)6.2H2O, the first-shell scattering of CDs/SnO2@Zn observed here at about 1.5 Å can be attributed to the characteristic Zn–O distance. According to EXAFS data, Zn2+ is seen to be bound to the CDs structure prior to the Zn deposition process taking place on the Zn surface.

In Fig. S13, both the XRD pattern and SEM image demonstrate highly crystalline nanoflowers: δ-MnO2 was successfully synthesized. Owing to its high theoretical capacity (308 mAh g−1) and good electrochemical stability, δ-MnO2 was chosen as the counter cathode material. In Fig. 5a, the charging and discharging processes of CDs/SnO2@Zn show a negative and positive shift, suggesting less polarizations. The density of states and Fermi level location are changed by the pyrrolic N dopants in CDs/SnO2@Zn, which add new electronic states to the anode. As a result, less energy is needed for electron transport during redox reactions, which causes the redox peaks of the CV curves to move and lower polarization. According to the galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) profiles, CDs/SnO2@Zn improved redox reaction kinetics. In Fig. 5b, CDs/SnO2@Zn reveals heightened storage capacity of 228 mAh g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1. The initial discharge capacity of the CDs/SnO2 Zn anode is larger than that of the bare Zn anode at 157 mAh g−1 in the full cell configuration.

N dopants in CDs/SnO₂@Zn introduce additional electronic states into the anode material's band structure, influencing the Fermi level's position and the density of states nearby. Consequently, the energy required for electron transfer processes during redox reactions is lowered, resulting in decreased polarization and shifts in the redox peaks of the CV curves. Furthermore, O functionalization in CDs/SnO₂@Zn alters the local electronic environment at the interface, generating new active sites for Zn ion adsorption and desorption. This modification increases ion accessibility, improves redox reaction kinetics, and reduces polarization. This observation suggests that the CDs/SnO₂@Zn SEI layer accelerates the anode's electrochemical activity by providing a favorable surface for charge transfer.

In Fig. 5c, the galvanostatic profiles of the CDs/SnO2@Zn‖ δ-MnO2 cell in the first, second, fifth, tenth and fiftieth cycles measured at a current density of 0.1 A g−1 are shown. These profiles correspond to the areal capacity of 1 A g−1. At the first discharge, a voltage plateau appeared at 1.1 V (vs. Zn/Zn2+), which is a characteristic feature of the δ-MnO2 cathode in AZIBs. Such a phenomenon occurred due to phase transformation i.e. so-called activation, induced by Mn dissolution. The Zn ion migration from δ-MnO2 to the anode represents the first charge at 1.7 V (vs. Zn/Zn2+). In the second cycle, such activation altered the discharge profile, resulting in the observation of three discharge periods, the lowest of which is still attributed to the activation of δ-MnO2. After the fifth cycle, this activation feature virtually disappeared, which is in line with earlier studies, indicating that the activation process had finished [3,53,54]. The voltage profiles in the second and fifth charge processes were the same as in the first charge process. Between these cycles, it appears that the Zn deposition was reversible. Similar phenomena are seen via CV measurements where the charging process is seen to be more reversible and the activation of δ-MnO2 lasts until the fifth cycle. The creation of Zn hydroxide sulphate (Zn4SO4(OH)6·5H2O) is indicative of the steep cathodic peak about 0.97 V in the first cycle, which is caused by the dissolution of δ-MnO2. In subsequent cycles, two cathodic peaks occurred at approximately 1.31 and 1.16 V, corresponding to the reversible redox interaction between ZHS and the activated ZnMn3O7·3H2O phase [55].

In Fig. 5d, the CDs/SnO2@Zn//δ-MnO2 and bare-Zn//δ-MnO2 cells underwent rate capability testing. The varying starting capacities of the two cells are linked to the activation of δ-MnO2. After activation at the fifth cycle, the practical starting capacities of CDs/SnO2@Zn//δ-MnO2 and bare-Zn//δ-MnO2 were found to be 226.12 and 136.12 mA h g–1, respectively. The cycle performance of bare-Zn//δ-MnO2 and CDs/SnO2@Zn//MnO2 cells was evaluated over 50 cycles at a voltage range of 1.0–1.8 V (vs. Zn/Zn2+), with different current densities.

In Fig. 5e, long cycling performance takes place at 200 mA g–1 for both electrodes in the full cells. Initially, the capacity at the fifth cycle was used to assess capacity retention due to the activation procedure. After 200 cycles, the capacity of the CDs/SnO2@Zn//δ-MnO2 and bare-Zn//δ-MnO2 cells decreased from 188.66 to 145.22 mA h g–1 and from 143.56 to 102.21 mA h g–1, respectively. However, N dopants in the CDs/SnO₂@Zn anode introduce additional electronic states into the anode material's band structure, influencing the Fermi level's position and the density of states nearby. Consequently, the energy required for electron transfer processes during redox reactions is lowered, resulting in decreased polarization. Notably, CDs/SnO₂@Zn enhances the electrochemical activity of the anode by providing a favorable surface for charge transfer. Furthermore, O functionalization in CDs/SnO₂@Zn alters the local electronic environment at the interface, creating new active sites for Zn ion adsorption and desorption. Therefore, the anode with CDs/SnO2@Zn//δ-MnO2 still exhibits higher stability. CDs/SnO2@Zn//δ-MnO2 and bare-Zn//δ-MnO2 recorded CE values of 98.9 %. and 98.3 %. Such high values confirming the impact of the SEI coating layer of CDs/SnO2@Zn//δ-MnO2 are particularly significant.

In this study, we successfully developed a tunable N-doped CDs/SnO2 artificial solid electrolyte interphase (ASEI) to stabilize the Zn anode, significantly enhancing the performance of AZIBs. By controlling the CDs/SnO2 ratio, we created distinct morphologies akin to bayberry-like and flower-like structures, enabling effective control of the electrode-electrolyte interface on the Zn anode. The CDs/SnO2 interface provided three key advantages:
1.
Zn adsorption is accelerated by nitrogen dopants, especially pyrrolic N, which produce surface dipoles and localized changes in the charge distribution. This results in lower adsorption barriers and easier transit of Zn ions to the nitrogen dopant sites.
2.
Hydroxyl oxygen dopants improve the hydrophilicity of the CDs/SnO2 layer, enhancing ion accessibility and generating robust adhesion to the Zn anode. This leads to dense nucleation sites for consistent Zn deposition.
3.
Non-covalent interactions create a de-solvation structure that accelerates the Zn ion transport channels, enhancing Zn2+ kinetics.

Consequently, the CDs/SnO2@Zn electrode demonstrated stable cycling for 1200 h at 2 mA cm−2, attaining 99.6 % CE in half cells, and full cells achieved 96.6 % CE over 800 h. These results indicate the effectiveness of our interface engineering approach. The straightforward synthesis method utilized carbon dot materials, making it suitable for practical applications. This work provides a viable strategy for developing high-performance AZIBs for large-scale energy storage applications.

All chemicals and materials were directly used without further purification, including citric acid, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid, tin (IV) chloride pentahydrate and urea. (ZnSO4·7 H2O) (≥99.0 %, Kemaus) and manganese (II) sulfate monohydrate (MnSO4·xH2O) (≥99.0 %, QREC) were used as metal salts for the battery electrolyte. N-Methyl pyrrolidone (NMP, MW 275000, GPC, AR) was purchased from Merck. Ethanol was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. and Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF, AR) was purchased from Merck. Carbon black (Super P) was purchased from TCI. Zn foil (thickness, 0.05 mm) and copper foil (thickness 0.05 mm) were used as substrates.

Nitrogen-doped CDs were prepared through a one-step hydrothermal method. Firstly, 2.102 g citric acid (carbon source) was dissolved in 60 mL of deionized (DI) water and 335μL ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (nitrogen source) was added, under stirring, until completely dissolved, and sonicated for 10 min. Then, the mixture was transferred to a 100 mL polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) autoclave. Temperature was maintained in an oil bath at 180◦C for 16 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the solution was filtered via a filter (0.25 μm) to remove impurities. Then, the solution was put under centrifugation (12,000 rpm), followed by dialysis retention (MWCO 1000 Dalton membrane). Finally, a clear dark yellow CDs solution was obtained and vacuum-dried at 80 °C overnight, turning into orange color CDs powder for further characterization.

Firstly, the precursors of SnO2: 0.2 M of SnCl4.5H2O, tin (IV) chloride pentahydrate and 0.04 M of urea were stirred for 10 h in an ambient atmosphere. By adding sodium hydroxide, the pH reached around 7. Next, 300 mg of CDs were further added to the above solution under continuous stirring for 1 h. Then, the mixed solution was poured into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave for 24 h at 180◦C. A yellowish powder (CDs@SnO2-1) was collected by centrifugation, washed with DI water and ethanol, several times, and dried under vacuum-drying at 60◦C. The powder obtained was finely ground and calcinated under 300◦C. For comparison, the greyish SnO2 powder was prepared with no addition of CDs powder. Likewise, CDs/SnO2-2 were obtained, using a similar method as previously: the N-CDs ratio increased to 600 mg. Finally, the material was heated in a tube furnace at 350 °C for 2 h with a ramping rate of 10 °C/min in an argon atmosphere in order to tune the morphology of N-CDs@SnO2.

For the synthesis of δ-MnO₂ to be employed as cathode material in battery cells, 0.948 g KMnO4 were dissolved in 35 mL of DI water. Subsequently, 0.169 g MnSO₄·H₂O were added to the solution. After stirring for 30 min, the mixture was transferred to an autoclave and subjected to hydrothermal treatment at 160 °C for 12 h in an oil bath. Post-synthesis, the autoclave was allowed to cool naturally at ambient temperature. The resultant product was then centrifuged, filtered and extensively washed by DI water and ethanol. Finally, the δ-MnO2 powder was dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C overnight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon dot interface layer on Zn anode
ce:section.ce:para: To fabricate the CDs/SnO2@Zn interface layer, a slurry was prepared by mixing polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) binder and the synthesized CDs/SnO2 composite in a weight ratio of 1:9, using N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP) as the solvent. The mixture was stirred thoroughly to achieve a homogenous consistency. Subsequently, the slurry was uniformly applied onto the surface of a bare Zn foil using a spin-coating technique. This method ensured the even distribution of the PVDF solution across the Zn surface.

The coated Zn foil was then dried overnight under vacuum conditions at 60°C to remove residual solvents. After drying, the resulting CDs/SnO2 interface layer was measured and was found to be 50 μm thick. The uniform coating formed a stable ASEI on the Zn anode, contributing to enhanced Zn plating/stripping behavior.
